posts.text:
🔧 The problem isn't just job displacement. Automation risks eroding meaningful work—the creativity, purpose, and social connections that make labor fulfilling.

Wiener warned of a "factory without employees" where humans compete with their own creations.

💡 In 1949, Wiener wrote to union leader Walter Reuther: "I do not wish to contribute to selling labor down the river."

His message? Tech isn't neutral. Its design must prioritize human well-being over profit.

📊 Key insights from the 2023 IEEE Workshop on Wiener's legacy:

• Automation benefits often concentrated, worsening inequality
• Regulation lags behind innovation
• Governance gaps in accountability persist

⚖️ Think the Luddites were anti-technology? Wrong. Their fight wasn't against machines—it was against systems that used tech to exploit workers.

Same patterns of displacement and "progress for the few" persist today.

🔮 Wiener understood we can't fully predict automation's ripple effects. The takeaway? We need ethical foresight as an ongoing process, not a one-time act.

Stay adaptable, proactive, and humble.

🌟 Automation doesn't have to mean dystopia. With the right frameworks, it can expand access to education/healthcare, reduce drudgery, and address inequalities.

But this requires rethinking success metrics—from profit to human flourishing.

How can we ensure automation enhances—not erodes—human dignity and purpose? Curious what you think—does this match what you're seeing in your work?

[LINK]

#Automation #FutureOfWork #AIGovernance #ResponsibleAI

linkedin.text:
Automation is reshaping the future of work—and Norbert Wiener saw it coming decades ago. [LINK]

🔧 The Challenge: Beyond Job Displacement
Automation isn't just about efficiency. It risks eroding meaningful work—the creativity, purpose, and social connections that make labor fulfilling. Wiener warned of a "factory without employees" where humans compete with their own creations.

💡 Wiener's Ethical Vision
In 1949, he wrote to union leader Walter Reuther: "I do not wish to contribute to selling labor down the river." His message was clear: technology isn't neutral. Its design must prioritize human well-being over profit.

📊 Key Insights from 2023 IEEE Workshop
• Automation benefits often concentrated, worsening inequality
• Regulation consistently lags behind innovation
• Persistent governance gaps in accountability
• Risk of reducing work to cold metrics rather than human values

🌟 The Opportunity
With the right frameworks, automation can expand access to education and healthcare, reduce drudgery, and address systemic inequalities. But this requires rethinking success metrics—moving from profit to human flourishing.

How can we ensure automation enhances rather than erodes human dignity and purpose? Happy to share details if useful.

[Add coauthors] | [Add funder]
GRAIL Center | Purdue University

#Automation #FutureOfWork #AIGovernance #ResponsibleAI #EthicsInTech #TechPolicy
